metadata.title:
Do YOU LONG TO OWN LAND? HERE ARE FREE RICH ACRES

Many Irrigated Farms to Be
Opened for Homesteading
in February on One Of
Roosevelt's Reclamation
PrOjects-Combination of
City and Farm Life Una
tilla Lands-Early Crops,
High Prices.

pages.raw_text:
4A1Su I TUGS.


LA GRANDE, Ore, Jan. 17.-One
hundred fine farms, rescued from
the desert by the wonders of irriga-
tion, will be open to homestead
entry under the reclamation act
after 8 a. m. February 10, at the
land office here.


Under notice already issued, en-
tries made hereafter for these
farms under the third unit of the
great Umatilla project shall be ac-
companied by application for water
rights and by the first installment
OF charges for the right, not less
than $3.50 per acre of irrigable
land, being first payment on the
building charge of SIS per acre and
$1.30 for operation and mainten-
ance for the first year.


The second installment of the
charge, not less than Sc per acre,
and the appropriate charge for op-
GRAFTON and maintenance, becomes
due on December / of each year
thereafter until paid.


The great Umatilla project is just
one of the many monuments to
Theodore Roosevelt's policies of
conservation, against which the
hand of Taft is lifted in Washing
ton. 1t is 100 miles east of Port
lang, along the Columbia river, and
between it and the Umatilla.


The soil is of great depth, sum-
mer comes early, and the growing
season is longer than in most parts
Of the west. The truck and fruit
grower on this project can probably
place his crops on the market in
advance of his competitors, and be
sure of top prices in the markets of
Spokane, Seattle, Tacoma and
Portland, all of which are tributary
by rail or water transportation.


The farms soon to be opened to
entry vary from 10 to 40 acres each.
During the past three years settle
ment has been going on rapidly in
completed parts of the project, and
considerable areas have been plant
ed in orchards and small fruits.


An interesting phase OF the de-
velopment here is the combination
Vi CItt anu country lllc, ldl illo are
SMALL many five and ten-acre or
chards are being laid out about the
towns, and the owners have built
their homes in town.


The water supply is the flood
flow of the Umatilla river, which is
stored in reservoir created by
constructing an earthen embank-
ment nearly 100 feet in height and
half mile long. Owing to the por-
GUS character of the soil many of
the canals are lined with cement.


The main line of the O. Il. & n.
railroad skirts the western edge of
the project, and the Spokane
branch runs through the northern
part. When the Cello locks are
completed the Columbia river will
furnish navigation to Portland and
Astoria. The main line of the
Northern Pacific railroad is on the
north bank of the river.
